name: Nachzehrer
description: The Nachzehrer, a creature of Germanic folklore, specifically prevalent in regions like Bavaria and Silesia, is a type of undead being distinct from the more commonly known vampire or zombie. Its name translates roughly to 'after-devourer' or 'one who feeds after death,' hinting at its macabre nature. Unlike vampires, Nachzehrers do not typically drink blood. Instead, they are believed to consume the life force of their living relatives, causing them to weaken and eventually die. This feeding is often blamed for epidemics and unexplained illnesses within a community. The Nachzehrer is thought to be created from individuals who died with unconfessed sins, grudges, or through unnatural means. Superstitions surrounding them were deeply rooted in beliefs about the restless dead and the potential for the deceased to harm the living. Prevention methods and remedies often involved specific burial rituals or post-mortem procedures to ensure the deceased could not return as a Nachzehrer.
subtitle: An undead creature that feeds on the living.
appearance: The appearance of a Nachzehrer varies. Some accounts describe them as looking like their corpse would, bloated and discolored. Others describe them as appearing mostly normal but with a gaunt or sickly look. They are sometimes said to have the ability to partially shift their form, becoming more monstrous when feeding or threatened. Some tales mention a shroud or burial cloth clinging to their form.
habitat: Nachzehrers are typically found in or near their graves, cemeteries, or places where they died. They are often confined to the area where they are buried, though their influence can extend beyond the immediate vicinity, affecting the health of those connected to them.
behavior: A Nachzehrer's primary behavior is to feed on the life force of the living. This feeding is often unconscious, with the Nachzehrer being driven by an insatiable hunger. They are not necessarily malevolent in a conscious way, but their actions have devastating consequences. They are also said to be able to communicate with the dead, potentially gaining knowledge or influence from the spirit world. Their activity is often blamed for outbreaks of disease and misfortune within the community.

keyMyths.title: The Silesian Plague
keyMyths.description: A small village in Silesia suffered a devastating plague. The villagers suspected a recently deceased man, known for his miserly ways, of being a Nachzehrer. They exhumed his body and found it to be bloated and seemingly untouched by decay. After decapitating the corpse and reburying it face down, the plague subsided.

keyMyths.title: The Baker's Curse
keyMyths.description: A baker who had cheated his customers was said to have returned as a Nachzehrer. His descendants fell ill one by one, their health mysteriously declining. A local priest performed an exorcism on the grave, and the baker's spirit was said to find rest, ending the family's suffering.

keyMyths.title: The Unquiet Grave
keyMyths.description: A young woman died suddenly, and her family believed she was being tormented by an unconfessed sin. They exhumed her body and found her shroud to be twisted around her neck. After untangling the shroud and performing a ritual of purification, the family found peace, believing the woman's spirit was finally at rest.
keyMyths.source: Folk Tales from the Black Forest

sources.author: Rose, Carol
sources.work: Giants, Monsters, and Dragons: An Encyclopedia of Folklore, Legend, and Myth
sources.description: Provides a general overview of the Nachzehrer within the context of Germanic folklore and undead creatures.
